coredata.dc:description: Membrane separation is a highly energy-efficient method for eliminating pollutants, ranging from micrometer-sized particles to angstrom-sized hydrated ions, from water. Nevertheless, the existing membrane technology, which uses costly synthetic materials, has become unsustainable for the most impoverished populations in the society. Thus, in the present study, a fully biobased sustainable functional membrane was developed for the effective separation of impurities from wastewater. A 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) oxidation and integration of carbon dots (CDs) to cellulose nanofiber (CNF), resulted in significant improvements in the functional properties of membranes. The effectiveness of this modification resulted in a remarkable augmentation in surface ζ-potential as –68.5 mV for pristine CNF to −102 mV for CDs blended CNF. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies confirmed the existence of essential functional groups that enabled better interactions within the composite membranes. The functionalization of CNF membranes resulted slightly lower in water flux from 1536 L/m2/h for pristine membranes and 1089 L/m2/h for TEMPOoxidized and 1402 L/m2/h for CDs integrated membranes. This hypothesis further confirmed that the tuned membranes were denser with a decreased pore size of 19.96 Å for pristine and 17.49 Å for TEMPO functionalized CNF membranes. We demonstrated that functionalized cellulose nanofiber membranes have high filtration efficiency for heavy metals (Fe3+, Cu2+), dyes (methylene blue and dyes from garment industry wastewater), and protein (bovine serum albumin). Moreover, such biobased composite membranes can be reused, thereby exerting a significant influence on the circular economy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Membrane separation is a highly energy-efficient method for eliminating pollutants, ranging from micrometer-sized particles to angstrom-sized hydrated ions, from water. Nevertheless, the existing membrane technology, which uses costly synthetic materials, has become unsustainable for the most impoverished populations in the society. Thus, in the present study, a fully biobased sustainable functional membrane was developed for the effective separation of impurities from wastewater. A 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) oxidation and integration of carbon dots (CDs) to cellulose nanofiber (CNF), resulted in significant improvements in the functional properties of membranes. The effectiveness of this modification resulted in a remarkable augmentation in surface ζ-potential as –68.5 mV for pristine CNF to −102 mV for CDs blended CNF. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies confirmed the existence of essential functional groups that enabled better interactions within the composite membranes. The functionalization of CNF membranes resulted slightly lower in water flux from 1536 L/m2/h for pristine membranes and 1089 L/m2/h for TEMPOoxidized and 1402 L/m2/h for CDs integrated membranes. This hypothesis further confirmed that the tuned membranes were denser with a decreased pore size of 19.96 Å for pristine and 17.49 Å for TEMPO functionalized CNF membranes. We demonstrated that functionalized cellulose nanofiber membranes have high filtration efficiency for heavy metals (Fe3+, Cu2+), dyes (methylene blue and dyes from garment industry wastewater), and protein (bovine serum albumin). Moreover, such biobased composite membranes can be reused, thereby exerting a significant influence on the circular economy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The availability of clean and pure water is a basic human right and it is an essential aspect of worldwide health and sustainable development [1]. Despite global promises and technological progress, more than 2.1 billion individuals globally remain lacking dependable access to clean water, thereby entrenching communities in cycles of poverty and disease [2,3]. Indeed, intense research activity is in progress to identify new robust materials and efficient approaches for water purification. Conventional methods, such as chemical precipitation, filtration, ion exchange, electrochemical treatment, membrane technologies, adsorption on activated carbon, evaporation, etc., are employed for the elimination of nanosized pollutants from water [4,5] are inefficient, and impractical to obtain substantial results. For example, the removal of metal ions by chemical precipitation or electrochemical treatment is ineffective when the ion concentration is between 1 and 100 mg/L [6]. Furthermore, with these methodologies, the introduction of new chemicals (i.e. acids and bases for pH adjustment, flocculating chemicals, coagulants, etc.) in the treated water is inevitable and can cause further problems. Other conventional approaches like ion exchange, membrane technologies, activated carbon adsorption, or evaporation are costly primarily because of the large energy consumption [7–9].

Indeed, the removal of contaminants, particularly nano-sized pollutants, needs reverse osmosis (RO) membranes with pore sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm require high energy input for the separation process [10–12]. To address these issues, there has been a significant transition towards novel separation technologies that ensure both efficiency and sustainability in water treatment systems.

Over the years, cellulose nanofibers (CNF) have become increasingly popular because of their distinctive features, including high aspect ratio, tensile strength, crystallinity, and elastic modulus. It is a biocompatible, nontoxic, biodegradable polymer that serves as an excellent substitute for synthetic, petroleum-based materials. CNFs are produced globally from various biomass sources, depending upon their availability [13]. Wood is likely the principal natural feedstock utilized for nanocellulose manufacturing. Due to easy accessibility, cost-effective processing, and superior properties, CNF serves as a crucial, environmentally friendly, next-generation building block for the development of advanced dimensional structures across many applications. Moreover, the adsorption capacity and steady network-forming ability of CNF render it a special material for the construction of diverse defined hybrid structures [14–16]. A novel strategy involved is the use of CNF-based “affinity membranes”, which provide a unique advantage in wastewater treatment owing to their inherent hydrophilic properties, superior biocompatibility, and tunable surface chemistry [17]. Furthermore, the unique separation mechanism i.e., adsorption followed by size-exclusion makes these membranes more advance compared to conventional membranes. In addition, the shift toward sustainable practices improves the value of renewable and biodegradable materials in the field of water purification.

Besides, the tuned surface of CNF can be easily used based on the specific pollutants being targeted, hence improving the efficiency of the membrane. The introduction of surface functional groups such as sulphonic, carboxylic, phosphate, and methyl enhances the performance of membranes in the separation of charged pollutants, including metal ions, dyes, viruses, proteins, and bacteria [18–21]. Definitely, affinity-based wastewater treatment systems exhibit minimal energy consumption because of their effective functioning, optimal inter-fiber spacing, and high wet strength. Also, various chemical and enzymatic functionalizations are reported in the literature [22,23] but functionalization of biobased composite membranes using carbon dots (CDs) could be a new approach for surface tuning as well as the implementation of such tuned membranes for water purification. CDs are one of the most popular and noticeable carbon nanomaterials due to their simple production technique. The chemical structure of CD can be readily modified by adjusting the synthesis conditions. Due to its customized functional groups, CD exhibits a strong affinity for different polymers, including CNF. There is a limited number of studies on integrating CDs in a CNF matrix by either physical or chemical methods [24,25].

Thus, this study deals with unique composite membranes for the separation of nanosized pollutants like dye, metal ions, and proteins using CNF-based affinity membranes implementing less energy and sustainable approaches. We propose that the membrane's filtration efficacy could be improved through unique two-step processes: first, TEMPO functionalization (responsible for the improved functional groups), and subsequently, integration of CDs (make the composite membranes more stable and effective in the separation process) to the CNF. Consequently, this improved functionality has the potential to produce a remarkably selective membrane distinguished by an improved surface, a notable negative ζ-potential, and essential wet stability for the uninterrupted flow of water in practical applications. Polar (–OH, –COOH) functional groups were incorporated into CNF via chemical catalysis, and the potency of the chemicals was examined concerning the modification of the CNF. To further examine the hypothesis, all synthesized membranes were subjected to testing with model water containing metal ions (Fe3+ and Cu2+), dyes, and protein. The filtration was conducted, and the impact of functionalization was assessed in relation to the separation efficiencies of membranes.

Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) derived from oil palm empty fruit bunch (EFB) of purity 99.9 % was purchased from Zoep Nano Sdn. Bhd., Malaysia. The chemicals, ferric chloride (FeCl3), copper chloride (CuCl2.H2O), sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate (NaH2PO4.H2O), sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate (NaH2PO4.7H2O), sodium chlorite (NaClO2) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were of analytical grades and acquired from ThermoFisher Scientific, USA. Sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) and hydrochloric acid (36 %), TEMPO (2,2,6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical; 98+%), sodium bromide (99+%), glacial acetic acid (99 %) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Thermo Scientific, USA. Garment industry wastewater (GIW) was collected in plastic containers from Thailand and kept at 4 °C in the dark for analysis. All other chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis and detailed characterization of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) have been described in our previous paper [26]. In brief, to synthesize N/S-CDs, 2.5 g of EFB (+50/−200 mesh) was introduced into the hydrothermal reactor with 50 mL of 0.4 mol L−1 p-phenylenediamine and 0.4 mol L−1 sodium thiosulfate to produce nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots. The hydrothermal reaction occurred at 220 °C for 6 h. Following the reaction, the material was recovered from the solution by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 15 min at 20 °C to separate the cellulose-rich fraction, followed by 10,000 rpm for separation of the CDs solution. The N/S-CDs were purified using a 0.22-μm syringe membrane filter before being dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1.0 kDa.

CNF was oxidized according to the process stated by Serra et al. utilizing 2,2,6,6,-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical (TEMPO)-NaBr-NaClO [27]. In short, 0.08 g TEMPO and 0.5 g NaBr were dissolved in 100 ml water containing 5.0 g CNF. To prepare sodium hypochlorite solution, the pH of NaClO solution (12 % w/v) was adjusted to pH 10 with 0.1 M HCl. TEMPO-mediated oxidation commenced with the addition of the appropriate quantity of NaClO solution (5.0 mM NaClO per gram of CNF). The pH of the reaction was sustained at pH 10 using 0.5 M NaOH until no more pH variation was detected. The TEMPO-oxidized CNF was repeatedly washed with ultrapure water and kept at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding of N/S-CDs to pristine and TEMPO-oxidized CNF
ce:section.ce:para: The binding of N/S-CDs to pristine CNF (P-CNF) and TEMPO-oxidized CNF (T-CNF) was carried out according to the procedure described in our previous paper [26]. The nomenclature CD-CNF and T-CD-CNF were assigned for N/S-CDs blended with pristine CNF and TEMPO-oxidized CNF, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pristine and functionalized CNF
ce:section.ce:para:
The ζ-potential of pristine, TEMPO-oxidized and N/S-CDs blended CNF was measured with Zeta-sizer (Malvern Nano ZS, Malvern, Herrenberg, Germany) at 25 °C. For homogeneous dispersion, CNF (0.01 % w/v) was ultrasonicated and vortexed before analysis. Three individual measurements were done to calculate the standard deviation and mean values of ζ-potential. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700, FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The measurements were conducted in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode in 400–4000 cm−1 range, with 64 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was determined using X-ray diffraction spectrometer (Bruker D8-Discover, Germany), equipped with nickel-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418) in the 2θ range of 10–80°. The crystallinity index (CrI) was calculated from the difference in XRD intensity between I200 (crystalline structure at 22.5°) and IAm (amorphous structure at 18°) divided by I200 as described in our earlier study [26] as shown in Equation (i).
(i)

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted using a PHI5000 VersaProbe II instrument developed by ULVAC-PHI in Kanagawa, Japan. The measurements took place at the SUT-NANOTEC-SLRI Joint Research Facility, Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI), Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. The X-ray source was monochromated Al-Kα at 1486.6 eV. The specimens were excited using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV. The survey spectra were obtained using an energy step of 1.000 eV and a pass energy of 117.4 eV. The high-resolution spectra were obtained using an energy step of 0.05 eV and a pass energy of 46.95 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The elongated cellulose fibers utilized in the membrane construction were EFB pulp, acquired from a pulp and paper industry in eastern Thailand. The cellulose fibers first recovered and purified from the pulp were then characterized for the composition of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, as well as the cellulose fiber length. The composition of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in the fiber was determined using the procedure outlined in our earlier publication [28]. The cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin contents after delignification and bleaching were 83 %, 5 %, and 1 %, respectively. The diameter of the cellulose fiber was assessed by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) analysis and showed an average range of 6–7 μm [28].

Hybrid CNF membranes were fabricated according to the procedure described in our previous paper [26]. Briefly, 0.5 % w/v of long EFB fibers and 0.5 % w/v of EFB nanofibers were mixed (1:1 ratio, total 1.0 % w/v) and then filtered through a vacuum-filtration device (100 ml of volume capacity). The prepared membranes after water removal were gently transferred on Teflon plates, then pressed between Teflon plates and dried in an oven at 40 °C for 48 h. Approximately 4.75 kg weight was applied on the Teflon plate for wrinkle-free membrane preparations. The blotting paper was placed over the membranes and changed every 10 h for fast drying. The membranes were stored at 37 °C in a moisture-free cabinet until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The pore size and distribution in membrane frameworks were assessed using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (Micromeritics ASAP 2000 equipment from Stockholm, Sweden). Samples were first chopped into fine pieces and degassed for 24 h at 80 °C in a dry N2 flow prior to measurements.

X-ray microtomography (XTM) experiment was performed to investigate the microstructures of the pristine and functionalized CNF membranes. XTM was carried out in Beamline 1.2W (BL 1.2W) at SUT-NANOTEC-SLRI Joint Research Facility, at Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI), Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. X-rays were generated from a 2.2-T multipole wiggler in the Siam Photon Source (Synchrotron Light Research Institute), operating at 1.2 GeV. A filtered polychromatic X-ray beam with a mean energy of 11.5 kV and a distance of 32 m from the source to the sample was used. The XTM experimental data were acquired by rotating the sample (0–180°) with an angular increment of 0.1° at a step size of 0.45° per second with an exposure time of 0.02 s. The acquired projection images for all the samples were preprocessed and normalized to correct the noise and beam non-uniformity. The captured data were preprocessed and reconstructed into 3D images with a filtered back projection algorithm using Octopus reconstruction software.

The water flux of the membrane structures was measured using a dead-end filtration cell (Sterlitech HP4750 Stirred cell, USA). Membranes were cut into circles with an area of 14.6 cm2. These circular membrane structures were placed in distilled water for at least 2 h so that the membrane completely swelled and then fixed between the designed module into the dead-end filtration cell to monitor the flow of water and adsorption/desorption buffer through the membranes. A pressure of 1.0 bar was exerted by the N2 supply to force the water and buffer to pass through the membrane structures. The precise measurement of water and buffer volume that flowed through the membrane structures during a certain period allowed for the calculation of membrane permeability, reported as Lh−1m−2 at the active filtration region (14.6 cm2) [29].

The surface wettability of the membrane was assessed by water contact angle measurements (KINO/SL200ks, USA) with the sessile drop method. Membranes were sectioned and affixed to the sample holder. Purified water was automatically applied onto the membranes, and high-resolution photographs of the water droplets were recorded at the moment they contacted the membrane surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Filtration performance of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes

ce:section.ce:para:
To measure iron and copper removal, synthetic wastewater that contained FeCl3 and CuCl2 was employed separately in a customized dead-end filtration cell. The known concentrations (100 ppm) of both the FeCl3 and CuCl2 in water were passed through the pristine and functionalized CNF membranes. The concentration of iron and copper in the feed and filtrate was determined using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), PerkinElmer Optima 8000 ICP-OES System (USA). The metal removal efficiencies of the membranes were calculated as shown in Equation (ii).
(ii)

The concentration of feed is denoted as Cf, whereas the concentration of permeate used in the measurements is denoted as Cp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF) analysis of metals
ce:section.ce:para: XRF is an analytical technique, that utilizes the characteristics of X-ray emission spectra generated by the sample. The binding and distribution of Fe3+ and Cu2+ onto the CNF membranes during the filtration were quantitively and qualitatively determined by XRF. Distribution and binding of these metal ions were directly determined in the solid phase (cellulose membranes) using a scanning wavelength-dispersion X-ray fluorescence microscope (Micro-XRF) HORIBA, XGT-9000, Kyoto, Japan. The spectrometer was equipped with an X-ray tube featuring a molybdenum anode and a lithium fluoride dispersion crystal. The tube was operated at a voltage of 30 kV and a current of 1000 μA.

The selection of methylene blue (MB) dye, which is extensively used in paper, pulp, and textile industries, was based on its varying average particle size, molecular structure, and core pigment type. These factors were considered to assess the removal efficiency of the membranes. A 5-ppm stock MB solution was made by dissolving in distilled water at pH 6.2. This solution was then used to flow in dead-end mode and get adsorbed. The percent removal capacity of the membranes was determined using equation (ii).

Real garment industry wastewater (GIW) was collected from the discharge site of the garment industry in Thailand. The concentrations of the various dyes present in the GIW were unknown. The sample was first filtered with Whatman filter paper and then diluted 10 times with distilled water. The prepared wastewater was filtered with different membranes using a dead-end filtration system and the removal efficiency was determined using the equations (ii).

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was filtered through membranes, and the adsorption capacity was assessed in dead-end flow mode. Membranes with a surface area of 14.6 cm2 were equilibrated with distilled water for 2 h before being placed in a dead-end filtration cell. This was followed by the filtration of 20 mM phosphate buffer of pH 7.4, containing dissolved proteins at a concentration of 1.0 g L−1. The membrane-bound proteins were removed by backflushing with the same phosphate buffer having 2 M NaCl. The regenerated membranes were equilibrated before the next filtration. All the filtration experiments were taking place at room temperature (25 ± 1 °C). The mass percentage and volumetric adsorption capacity were assessed concurrently. Following each filtration, aliquots of the filtrate were examined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1800, Kyoto, Japan). The concentration of protein in the feed, filtrate, and bound to membranes was determined by the Bradford method of protein estimation [30] and the protein removal capacity (Q) was calculated using Equation (iii).

Where Vo represents the volume (L) of the protein solution utilized, W denotes the dry weight of the membrane, and Ci and Ct indicate the concentrations (g L−1) of the protein solutions before and after filtration.

The pristine and functionalized CNF was characterized by various analytical techniques. The selected ‒COO‾ group was introduced onto CNF through chemical oxidation (TEMPO-oxidation) while the coupling of N/S-CDs was taking place through non-covalent interaction between N/S-CDs and CNF. CNF without modification is referred to as pristine CNF (P-CNF), whereas functionalized CNF such as TEMPO functionalized CNF, CDs bound CNF, and TEMPO functionalized CDs bound CNF are named as T-CNF, CD-CNF, and T-CD-CNF, respectively. Similar nomenclatures were used for membrane frameworks also as P-CNF-M, T-CNF-M, CD-CNF-M, and T-CD-CNF-M for pristine CNF, TEMPO functionalized CNF, CDs bound CNF, and TEMPO functionalized CDs bound CNF membranes, respectively.

FTIR spectroscopy was done to confirm the TEMPO oxidation of CNF. In Fig. 1 A, the stretching vibration of the free hydroxyl group (‒OH) is shown by a broad peak around 3200-3400 cm−1 [31], while the C‒H symmetric and asymmetric vibrations in the cellulose molecule are represented by a large peak around 2800˗2900 cm−1 [32,33]. The FTIR spectra of P-CNF and T-CNF displayed cellulose fingerprint region at 1400–850 cm−1. FTIR spectra of TEMPO functionalized CNF show characteristic bands at 1027, 1370, and 1410 cm−1 (Fig. 1A) which denote stretching and bending vibrations of (C-O-C), (C–H), and (–CH2) bonds in the cellulosic matrix, respectively. The peak at 896 cm−1 corresponds to cellulosic β-glycosidic linkages [34,35]. A strong absorption band at 1606 cm−1 in TEMPO functionalized CNF represents the carboxylate group (‒COO‾) that has been introduced by TEMPO-mediated oxidation [36,37]. The addition of negatively charged ‒COO‾ groups significantly enhances the effective dispersion of CNFs in water through electrostatic repulsion [38]. These findings demonstrate that the carboxyl group is effectively integrated into CNFs following TEMPO oxidation.

Crystalline properties of pristine and TEMPO-modified CNF were examined using XRD patterns and the result is shown in Fig. 1 (B). The acquired data demonstrated that the cellulose I crystal displayed characteristic XRD patterns at 2θ of 14.8, 16.4, 22.6, and 34.2 are indexed as (110), (110), (200), and (040) planes [39]. The presence of diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22.50 and 16.50 in this study points to 1ī0 and 110 lattice planes of cellulose [40,41]. The XRD patterns of P-CNF exhibited cellulose-I crystal structure that had not changed even after the oxidation by TEMPO/NaBr/NaClO (Fig. 1B), indicating the oxidation resistance of cellulose I crystals. The crystallinity index (CrI) of pristine and TEMPO functionalized CNF was calculated using the X-ray diffraction pattern, and the result is shown in Table 1 . The CrI of P-CNF (50.32 %) was slightly higher than T-CNF (46.57 %). Rohaizu and Wanrosli supported a lowered crystallinity index after the TEMPO oxidation reaction [41]. Apart from the surface-exposed hydroxymethyl groups on cellulose crystals, certain inner hydroxymethyl groups partially engaged in the oxidation process. This might lower the direct TEMPO-oxidized cellulose's crystallinity [42].

The ζ-potential of pristine and functionalized CNF was measured and reported in Fig. 1C and Table 1. Pristine CNF displayed the lowest ζ-potential as −68.4 ± 15.2 mV. An increase in ζ-potential (−89.6 ± 6.6 mV) was observed when pristine CNF oxidized via TEMPO oxidation. Further increment in ζ-potential (−102.8 ± 0.6 mV) was noticed when N/S-CDs were combined with the P-CNF. However, ζ-potential (−90.8 ± 10.5 mV) was slightly lowered for T-CD-CNF, although this value was higher than the pristine and T-CNF. Therefore, the ζ-potential of all four CNFs was in the pattern of CD-CNF > T-CD-CNF > T-CNF > P-CNF. These results demonstrated that the decrease in ζ-potential of T-CD-CNF might be linked with the weak interaction of negatively charged N/S-CDs with the T-CNF as it already has a significant number of negative charges. Therefore, the binding of negatively charged N/S-CDs to the T-CNF might reduce due to electrostatic repulsion. Highly negative surface ζ-potential (−89.6 ± 6.6 mV) was measured when CNF was oxidized using TEMPO since the conversion of –OH to –COOH (hydroxyl to carboxylic groups) took place at carbon six position of cellulose [29,43].

Fig. 1D depicts the XPS survey spectra of pristine and functionalized CNF. All nanofiber samples exhibit two prominent peaks with binding energies of 284.8 eV and 531.5 eV, indicating the presence of C 1s and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 1D). Besides these two peaks (C 1s and O 1s), two additional peaks at 168.3 and 399.9 eV binding energies were reported in CD-CNF and T-CD-CNF presenting the S 2p and N 1s, respectively (Fig. 1D). The presence of S 2p and N 1s in CDs bound CNF is because of the N/S doping onto CDs which further bound to CNF. To get detailed information about changes in the types of carbon-oxygen bonds, C 1s peaks of the pristine and T-CNF were deconvoluted into several peaks, as depicted in Fig. 1E and F, respectively. The comparative quantities of various carbon and oxygen functional groups in each sample, derived from the curve fitting of the XPS spectra are shown in Table S1. The absence of hydroxyl groups at the C4 position indicates that reactions at this site would have the least impact on the carboxyl content. However, TEMPO improves sensitivity for C6 oxidation, resulting in an increased carboxylate content. This explanation is reinforced by the curve fitting analysis. The C 1 s peaks might be precisely illustrated by three peaks in both the pristine and T-CNF. The C 1 s1 peak is associated with C–C (or C–H) and exhibits a low energy of binding as 284.5 ± 1 eV. The C 1s2 peak at 286.4 ± 1 eV is linked with carbon bonded to a single oxygen, –C–O–, while the C 1s3 signal at 287.5 ± 1 eV is attributed to –C=O or O–C–O (Fig. 1E). The T-CNF exhibited an additional curve matching the carboxylate group, with the O–C=O peak at the maximum binding energy of 287.8 ± 1 eV, representing C 1s4 (Fig. 1F). Peak patterns of CD-CNF were the same as EFB-CNF while T-CD-CNF displayed a similar peak pattern to T-CNF (Fig. S1).

It can be summarized that TEMPO and NS/CDs functionalization of pristine CNF significantly enhanced the negative charge and additional binding sites in the membranes. This improved the efficacy of membranes for wastewater containing heavy metals and dyes, which are frequently challenging to remove with conventional filtration techniques. This targeted removal is particularly advantageous for addressing intricate wastewater flows including many pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced characterization of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes
Porosity of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The porosity, pore volume, and specific surface area of membrane frameworks were analyzed using the BET technique (Fig. 2 A and B). Table 2 provides a summary of the membranes' attributes. The specific surface area of membrane frameworks ranged from 0.98 to 2.02 m2 g−1, with the highest specific surface area for T-CNF-M at 2.02 m2 g−1, and the lowest measured for T-CD-CNF-M at 0.98 m2 g−1. The surface area examined in this work corresponds with previous findings addressing the architectures of nanocellulose membranes [26,44,45].

The specific surface area and pore volume of the membranes have been demonstrated to vary depending on the concentration of CNF, drying conditions, and chemical functionalization. Gebald et al. reported that the BET surface area a of freeze-dried nano-fibrillated cellulose were recorded as 26.8 m2 g−1, while the amine-functionalized nano-fibrillated cellulose exhibited a BET surface area of only 7.1 m2 g−1 [46]. Therefore, a decrease in BET surface area was recorded when the freeze-drying method was employed for adsorbent synthesized using amine-modified nanofibrillated cellulose. A decrease in the pore size with increasing concentration of functional materials was documented in previous studies [47,48].

Present work engaged as fabrication of membranes using a vacuum filtration process, followed by hot pressing at 40 °C. The low specific surface area of CNF membranes might be because of the hydrogen bonding during vacuum filtration and drying. The pore dimensions and distribution offer further validation of the confirmed data. The membrane's pore size distributions were determined by the Kelvin equation and the BJH method. The measured pore size ranged from 13 to 20 nm, with T-CD-CNF-M exhibiting the smallest pore size of 13.54 Å, while the largest pore size of 19.96 nm was recorded for the pristine membrane. The pore diameters of membranes were in the order of P-CNF-M > CD-CNF-M > T-CNF-M > T-CD-CNF-M (Table 2). The decrease in pore size facilitated the TEMPO oxidation of CNF, hence enhancing the -COOH groups in cellulose. Increased carboxyl group thus increases the interactions between the CNF via hydrogen bonding which ultimately decreases the pore size of the TEMPO functionalized CNF membranes.

Water permeability of the pristine and functionalized membranes was assessed using a dead-end stirred cell filtration system at a pressure of 1.0 bar, controlled by supplied N2 gas. In dead-end filtration, water flows perpendicularly to the membrane surface, trapping particles on the filter. Prior to flux measurement, each membrane was immersed in water for at least 2 h to ensure complete swelling and saturation. Flux was measured with distilled water, 20 mM phosphate buffer pH 7.4, and desorption buffer (20 mm phosphate buffer with 2 M NaCl). All the tested membranes demonstrated peak permeability in distilled water, succeeded by phosphate-buffer solution, and subsequently by phosphate-buffer-NaCl solution, as illustrated in Fig. 2C. The lower permeation of phosphate and phosphate-NaCl buffer might be allied with the swelling of cellulosic fibers and the contraction of the overall composite membrane network [49–51]. The surface tension of the liquid and the membrane's surface energy play a critical role in the flow rate of the fluid across the membranes. At beginning, only the bigger channels become saturated while filtering, since the binding forces among water molecules surpass the adhesive forces at the liquid-solid interface, rendering the smaller pores inactive in the process. Extended filtration time enables tiny pores to enhance membrane filtration efficiency. The maximum permeability was recorded for pristine membrane with distilled water (1535.95 ± 134.6 Lm− 2h−1), followed by CD-CNF-M (1402.39 ± 92.46 Lm− 2h−1). The minimum water flux was documented with phosphate-NaCl buffer for T-CNF-M (729.45 ± 32.95 Lm− 2h−1) (Fig. 2C). The results obtained are corroborated by a BET analysis, which revealed that the pristine membranes exhibited considerably larger specific surface area and pore diameter compared to the T-CNF-M. The reduced water flow rate of the modified membranes (T-CNF-M and T-CD-CNF-M) can be attributed to the fact that the CNF becomes finer during TEMPO oxidation, resulting in a stronger network structure. This, in turn, leads to a decrease in both the volume and width of the pores.

Hydrophilic characteristics of the membranes were assessed via water contact angle measurement, which is influenced by the substrate's surface morphology and chemical composition [52]. The impact of membrane functionalization on membrane characteristics was analyzed by examining the static contact angles of membranes before and following functionalization. Pristine membranes exhibit a higher contact angle of 58.5° compared to functionalized membranes, which have contact angles of 27.5° for T-CNF-M, 43.8° for CD-CNF-M, and 31.3° for T-CD-CNF-M, respectively (Fig. 2D). These findings indicate an enhancement in hydrophilicity/wettability post-modification, likely due to an increase in the content of functional groups (–COO‾) following the modification. Our findings corroborated those of a previously published study that examined the contact angle of TEMPO-oxidized membranes with varying concentrations of carboxylic groups. A reduction in contact angle was associated with an increase in –COO‾ content. The primary disadvantage of CNF-based membranes is their propensity to swell in water, which compromises their sieving efficacy and makes them unsuitable for the separation of tiny molecules such as dyes or metal ions. Consequently, affinity membranes are typically preferred [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray microtomographic characterization of pristine and functionalized CNF membranes

ce:section.ce:para: The physical properties of different species applied for the filtration and evaluation of membrane efficiencies are shown in Table 4 . A solution of iron chloride (FeCl3) in deionized water at a concentration of 100 ppm (100 mg L−1) corresponding to strongly iron-contaminated water was used to determine membrane filtration efficiencies. The molecules that were filtered through CNF membranes had positive zeta potentials for FeCl3, CuCl2, and methylene blue (Table 4). On the other hand, BSA and garment industry wastewater had negative zeta potentials. These results demonstrated that charged molecules were engaged in electrostatic interactions/repulsions with the membranes, hence affecting the membrane's filtration efficiency. For measurements, membranes were installed in a dead-end cell, an iron solution was passed through membranes and the permeate fractions were collected. The presence of a haze on the membrane surface signifies that the membrane had filled with iron, hence demonstrating the membrane's ability to remove iron (Fig. S2). The iron concentration in each permeate fraction was measured using ICP-OES, and the filtration efficiency of membranes was calculated and shown in (Fig. 4 A).

P-CNF-M displayed minimum removal efficiency (25.26 ± 1.4 %), whereas the T-CNF-M possesses the highest iron removal efficiency (51.85 ± 5.7 %). The primary reason for enhanced Fe3+ removal efficiency of the functionalized membrane is likely the augmentation of carboxylate groups and surface ζ-potential of CNF due to TEMPO oxidation. The structures of the T-CD-CNF membrane exhibit the second highest percentage of iron removal (48.29 ± 5.6 %), yet have a lower ζ-potential compared to the CD-CNF-M. Thus, it can be inferred that both membrane functionalization and tunning are the key factors that are allied with the high efficiency of iron removal. Iron removal capacity with the membrane structures was in the order of T-CNF-M > T-CD-CNF-M > CD-CNF-M > P-CNF-M. Iron removal capacity is a quantitative analysis based on the original concentration. These results are similar to previous studies where phosphorylated nanocellulose bio-adsorbents effectively remove metal ions [55] and microfibrillated cellulose-based membrane was applied for ferric ions removal [29].

Ingestion of copper can cause acute poisoning to the liver and kidneys that damages these vital organs. It stimulates the central nervous system and contact with some of its compounds may induce cancer. It has been documented that the introduction of N group in the modified CNF structure enhanced the active adsorption sites around each particle that are effective in copper removal through their electron pairs. In this study, pristine and functionalized CNF membranes were fabricated for the enhanced copper ion removal from the wastewater. According to the results presented in Fig. 4B, the T-CNF-M had the highest percentage of copper removal 29.31 ± 0.08 %, and P-CNF-M showed the lowest amount of copper removal (12.87 ± 0.14 %). The copper removal efficiencies of CD-CNF-M and T-CD-CNF-M were 15.78 ± 0.26 % and 28.64 ± 0.09 %, respectively. Considering these results, it can be concluded that functionalized membranes lead to their beneficial effect in improving the efficiency of copper removal. By selecting TEMPO-CNF-M as the best-performing membrane in the removal and regeneration of copper. A study utilized TEMPO-oxidized cellulose and chitin nanofibers as sorbents for enhanced adsorption and removal of heavy metal ions from contaminated water [56].

X-ray fluorescence (XRF) mapping and spectral analysis were carried out to measure the adsorption of iron and copper on pristine and modified CNF membranes (Fig. 5 ). Elemental analysis of membranes after metal filtration was determined and the obtained data are shown in Table 1. XRF spectra of metals adsorbed on the pristine and modified membranes revealed the peaks about binding energies corresponding to the atoms of carbon, oxygen, and copper/iron. The pristine and functionalized membranes consist of elements C, O, Fe, and Cu. The amount of iron adsorbed on the membranes was 0.212 wt%, 1.43 wt%, 0.03 wt%, and 0.32 wt% for P-CNF-M, T-CNF-M, CD-CNF-M, and T-CD-CNF-M, respectively. However, the amount of copper adsorbed on the membranes was 0.04 wt% for P-CNF-M, 0.51 wt% for T-CNF-M, 0.03 wt% for CD-CNF-M, and 0.05 wt% for T-CD-CNF-M. The presence of iron and copper on the membranes confirmed the successful binding of metals with the membranes. Regarding the ratio of these metals on the membranes, their atomic percentage was approximately the same, but because of higher molar mass atoms compared to carbon, hydrogen and oxygen atoms showed higher weight percentages. Adsorption of iron on the membranes can be seen as presented in Fig. S2 as iron gets oxidized and presents as brownish color on membranes. All these analysis results confirmed the successful adsorption of iron and copper on the CNF membranes.

Membrane filtration is an emerging technology for the removal of pollutants from wastewater. The advantages of the nano-filtration system, in contrast to reverse osmosis, are linked to reduced operating costs while maintaining an acceptable yield in pollutant removal. In this study, methylene blue (MB), a common pollutant from the textile, and paper and pulp industry was treated with pristine and functionalized CNF membranes. The obtained data demonstrated that the highest MB removal was 94.01 ± 0.20 % with the T-CD-CNF-M followed by the CD-CNF-M (80.30 ± 0.82 %). The pristine membrane showed the lowest MB removal at 65.52 ± 1.75 % while the T-CNF-M exhibited 79.25 ± 0.97 % MB removal under the same experimental condition (Fig. 4D). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of MB before and after filtration exhibited a reduction in intensities over the 450-750 nm range (Fig. S3). The MB spectra exhibited peak absorption at 668 nm. The untreated MB had a dark blue color which changed to a light blue shade after filtration (Fig. S3). The decline in color intensities after filtration resulted in the binding of dye to the membranes which was confirmed by the dark blue color of the CNF membrane after filtration as shown in Fig. S3. The presence of carboxyl and amine groups in the T-CD-CNF-M leads to high MB removal as MB is a positively charged molecule, whereas the membranes display negative charges due to the presence of hydroxy, carboxyl, and amine functional groups. Therefore, electrostatic attractions significantly influence the interactions between the cationic groups of the positively charged dyes and the anionic groups in CNF membranes. Moreover, various hydrogen bond interactions between the hydroxyl groups of CNF membranes and different groups of the dye may further augment the adsorption mechanism.

Several studies demonstrate the filtration efficiency of pristine and functionalized cellulose nanomaterials in the adsorption and removal of dyes from wastewater. For instance, CNF derived from the kenaf core was utilized to adsorb cationic MB dye [57]. The enhancement of the adsorption capacity of cellulose nanocrystals through carboxylation has been documented for various cationic dyes, including basic fuchsin, crystal violet, and malachite green [58]. The efficacy of copper ion and direct red-16 removal from wastewater has been enhanced through the application of a membrane composed of polyether sulfone (PES) as the matrix and amine-functionalized cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) as the nanofiller [59].

In addition to filtering a known concentration of MB from synthetic wastewater, we utilized actual garment industry wastewater treatment, and the results are presented in Fig. 4C. The maximum dye removal from GIW wastewater was 25.41 ± 1.27 % with the T-CNF-M. The dye removal capacities for P-CNF-M, CD-CNF-M, and T-CD-CNF-M were 13.51 ± 0.67 %, 19.26 ± 0.96 %, and 20.1 ± 1.0 %, respectively. The efficacy of membranes for dye filtration from GIW was considerably lower than that for MB, perhaps because of various reasons. The dropped filtration efficacy of GIW may be attributed to the weak affinity of dyes with the filtering membranes since some differences with varying charges result in competing adsorption, charge repulsion, and potential aggregation, which could impair overall filtration efficacy. Furthermore, the alkaline (pH 10.3) nature of the GIW may alter the properties of charged molecules thus lowering the strength of membrane and dye interactions. The reported results of dye removal in this work were in accordance with the results reported in previous studies [60–62].

The initial stage in evaluating the protein filtration and performance of membranes was to determine their permeability with distilled water and buffers, as detailed in the materials and methods section. Protein was solubilized in phosphate buffer solution and filtered using a dead-end filtration technique to evaluate their adsorption/desorption performance (Fig. 6 ). The filtration efficiency order of the membranes for the protein was T-CNF-M (∼29 %) > T-CD-CNF-M (∼25 %) > CD-CNF-M (∼24 %) > P-CNF-M (∼23 %), while the surface ζ-potential order of the composite membranes was CD-CNF > T-CD-CNF > T-CNF > P-CNF, indicating the predominant influence of structural modifications. The surface morphology of T-CNF-M for pre- and post-protein filtrations was studied with a scanning electron microscope (Fig. S4).

It has been demonstrated that BSA is a negatively charged protein, showing reduced binding affinity with membranes, which are also negatively charged. These results further validate the influence of surface charges, specifically functional tuning played a dominant role in the separation of species with the same and opposite charges. Additionally, the desorption using 2 M NaCl resulted in an excellent recovery; the minor losses may be due to assay error or a small fraction of irreversibly bound proteins. The binding of BSA to the membranes occurred at a relatively low level (<10 %), possibly through non-specific hydrophobic interactions [63]. The variations among these values were also influenced by additional forces involved in protein adsorption, such as hydrogen bonding, predominantly occurring between –N atoms of the protein, and –OH groups of the membranes which is predominant in cellulose. Therefore, in conjunction with charge selectivity, this also accounts for the random adsorption capacity of proteins in membranes with varying structures. The hydrodynamic dimension of a protein depends on its molecular weight and tertiary structure, influenced by pK values. Variations in net charge at specific pH levels and the presence of buffer molecules may alter hydration and hydrodynamic volume (size and shape) related to spherical structures, thereby affecting electrostatic interactions with membrane surfaces. We assert that the membrane, specifically T-CNF-M, is highly effective for the separation of the protein (Fig. 6B) due to the predominance of structural adjustment. Initially, all membranes exhibit high percentage removal, confirming the predominant influence of structural tuning over functional tuning, suggesting that narrowing the pore-size distribution is more significant than the introduction of functional groups.

This study presents customized hybrid CNF membranes and their impact on the removal of heavy metals, proteins, and dyes. To evaluate our method, functional membranes were synthesized using TEMPO oxidation, resulting in a high density of carboxylic groups and the integration of CDs onto the composite membranes. Four different composite membranes are utilized for the removal of diverse charged and neutral species. The T-CNF-M and T-CD-CNF-M exhibit higher efficiency for dye separation relative to the P-CNF-M and CD-CNF-M, owing to the enhanced charge density of the -COO‾ group. Moreover, functionalized membranes dominate all separation studies, confirming that the structural change significantly influences the separation of metal ions, proteins, and dyes, which indeed relies on the pore-size distribution of the membranes. Consequently, the processing methods significantly influence application, as evidenced by the substantial removal percentage of targeted species by T-CNF-M and T-CD-CNF-M. This offers them intriguing as a cost-efficient, conveniently accessible, disposable , and alternative approach in water filtration applications. We anticipate this result will be applicable in various industrial discharged wastewater for the targeted removal of charged molecules. We imply that renewable cellulosic membranes will be pivotal in future water purification applications.
